title: On the role of magnesium ions in RNA stability
abstract:
On the Role of Magnesium
Ions in RNA Stability
Vinod K. Misra
David E. Draper
Department of Chemistry,
Johns Hopkins University,
Baltimore, MD 21218
Received and accepted 4 March 1999
Abstract:
Divalent cations, like magnesium, are crucial for the structural integrity and biological
activity of RNA. In this article, we present a picture of how magnesium stabilizes a particular folded
form of RNA. The overall stabilization of RNA by Mg21 is given by the free energy of transferring
RNA from a reference univalent salt solution to a mixed salt solution. This term has favorable
energetic contributions from two distinct modes of binding: diffuse binding and site binding. In
diffuse binding, fully hydrated Mg ions interact with the RNA via nonspeciﬁc long-range electro-
static interactions. In site binding, dehydrated Mg21 interacts with anionic ligands speciﬁcally
arranged by the RNA fold to act as coordinating ligands for the metal ion. Each of these modes has
a strong coulombic contribution to binding; however, site binding is also characterized by sub-
stantial changes in ion solvation and other nonelectrostatic contributions. We will show how these
energetic differences can be exploited to experimentally distinguish between these two classes of
ions using analyses of binding polynomials. We survey a number of speciﬁc systems in which
Mg21–RNA interactions have been studied. In well-characterized systems such as certain tRNAs
and some rRNA fragments these studies show that site-bound ions can play an important role in
RNA stability. However, the crucial role of diffusely bound ions is also evident. We emphasize that
diffuse binding can only be described rigorously by a model that accounts for long-range electro-
static forces. To fully understand the role of magnesium ions in RNA stability, theoretical models
describing electrostatic forces in systems with complicated structures must be developed.

Keywords:
divalent cations; magnesium; RNA; ion binding; RNA folding
INTRODUCTION
RNA folds into a variety of complex shapes required
for its biological activities. These structures are built,
in large part, upon a set of three-dimensional archi-
tectural motifs that are only now being described.1–4
Understanding the complex interplay of forces that
stabilize a particular RNA structure remains an elu-
sive problem—“the RNA folding problem.” The main
free energy contributions to RNA stability can be
divided into electrostatic and nonelectrostatic terms.
The electrostatic contribution includes the interaction
of the nucleic acid charges with other charges on the
macromolecule, the surrounding solvent, and ions in
solution. The nonelectrostatic contribution includes
hydrophobic interactions, van der Waals interactions,
and translational, rotational, vibrational, and conﬁgu-
rational entropies.5,6 Though the relative magnitude
of each of these contributions to RNA stability is not
well deﬁned, it is clear that electrostatic forces play a
particularly important role in both the stability and
interactions of RNA. The strong electrostatic repul-

 sions between closely packed phosphates in a com-
pactly folded RNA must be reduced by counterions.
As a result, the structural integrity and biological
activity of RNA strongly depends on the type and
concentration of cations in solution.7,8
Both monovalent and divalent ions can effectively
stabilize RNA. For example, it has been known for
almost thirty years that monovalent cations such as
Na1 are important for stabilizing the tertiary structure
of tRNA.9 More recently, the selective binding of
monovalent cations has been shown to inﬂuence the
stability and activity of other RNAs, including the
Terahymena group I intron and ribosomal RNAs.10,11
However, the unique role of magnesium ions has
always been particularly evident. Many early studies
clearly showed that Mg21 strongly stabilized the na-
tive tertiary structure of tRNA even in the presence of
a large excess of monovalent ions.9 Since then, mag-
nesium ions have been shown to be important for the
speciﬁc structure and function of many RNAs, includ-
ing the hammerhead ribozyme, hairpin ribozyme, Tet-
rahymena group I intron, group II intron, and ribonu-
clease P ribozyme.12,13 The ongoing challenge has
been to understand how these cations stabilize a par-
ticular folded form of RNA.
THE CONCEPT OF ION BINDING
The strong attractive interactions between cations and
nucleic acids are generally represented by the forma-
tion of different bound states in solution.14 In analogy
to protein solutions,15 bound states in RNA systems
are often split into two distinct classes depending on
the strength of their interaction with the RNA: dif-
fusely bound and site bound.16 In diffuse binding,
both univalent and divalent salts interact with the
strong anionic ﬁeld around the nucleic acid backbone
via nonspeciﬁc long-range Coulombic interactions
through solvent.17 As a result, a high local concentra-
tion of cations (e.g., Na1 or Mg21) accumulates
around all nucleic acids, forming a “delocalized”
counterion atmosphere. In some cases, high concen-
trations of these diffusely bound ions may be local-
ized in a relatively small volume with high electro-
static potential, but there is no signiﬁcant barrier to
their translational motion around the nucleic acid.18
As a further consequence of this relatively weak in-
teraction between the cations and the RNA, the hy-
dration shells surrounding the ion and the RNA re-
main intact (Figure 1a). The counterion atmosphere
plays a crucial role in stabilizing speciﬁc RNA struc-
tures by “screening” the repulsion between neighbor-
ing phosphates. The extent to which these counterions
bind and stabilize a nucleic acid is directly propor-
tional to its overall charge density as well as the
counterion concentration.17,19 Since the interaction of
delocalized counterions with a polyelectrolyte does
not involve a ﬁxed number of speciﬁc sites (i.e., there
is no characteristic saturability), it cannot be de-
scribed generally by a mass-action law20; only an
electrostatic theory can be used to describe the inter-
action of diffusely bound counterions with a polyion
under a given set of conditions.

binding in which the solvation shell (dotted line) of the ion
and its phosphate ligand remain intact; (B) outer sphere
complex in which the ion and its ligand share their solvation
shells; (C) inner sphere complex in which the ion and its
ligand make direct contact without intervening waters. Mg
ion is green; water molecules are blue; phosphorous atom is
yellow; and oxygen atoms are red.

In site binding, ions are trapped near the surface of
the RNA when their thermal translational energy is
not sufﬁcient to overcome strong local attractive
forces.15 Ions can be localized on the surface of the
RNA in two different ways, distinguished by their
hydration state (Figures 1b and 1c).21 The ﬁrst type of
site binding is sometimes termed an “outer-sphere”
complex in which the ions are bound in the sense ﬁrst
described by Bjerrum for concentrated simple elec-
trolytes.22 The strong electrostatic ﬁeld surrounding
nucleic acids results in the accumulation of cations in
pockets of negative electrostatic potential created by
the irregular shape of the molecular surface.23–26
Some of these counterions become kinetically and
thermodynamically “trapped” in these electrostatic
potential wells and are unable to continue their ran-
dom motion. The key physical feature of these “elec-
trostatically bound” ions is the disruption of all but the
innermost hydration layer, so that the ion and a nearby
RNA ligand share solvation shells (Figure 1b). It is
difﬁcult to distinguish these ions energetically from
completely delocalized ions that remain fully hy-
drated in an electrostatic potential well.22,27
In the second type of site binding, an ion strongly
interacts with the RNA by directly contacting a set of
RNA ligands with no intervening waters (Figure 1c).
Such an ion and its site is sometimes called an “inner
sphere” complex, like the corresponding state for a
divalent metal ion and a sulfate anion.28 The dehy-
dration of these site bound ions is a fundamental
energetic feature of this type of binding. The very
large cost of dehydration must be offset by both
electrostatic and nonelectrostatic interactions between
the ion and the RNA. In analogy to ion-pair formation
in electrolyte solutions, both outer and inner sphere
interactions between site bound ions and a discrete
number of sites can be described by an association
constant Ka, which obeys the laws of mass action.22
The association constant for ion pair formation in
simple electrolytes, Ka has the form27,29

A basic goal of this review is to provide a general
framework for discussing experimental and theoreti-
cal work that tries to understand how each of these
different ion binding modes contributes to RNA fold-
ing. We will focus on the role of magnesium ions in
particular. In the ﬁrst part of the review, a thermody-
namic model for discussing the energetics of ion–
RNA interactions will be presented. We will then
discuss a number of experimental approaches that
have been used to quantify divalent ion binding to
RNA. Finally, we will review a number of speciﬁc
systems in which Mg21–RNA interactions have been
An important point that will be developed is that it
is experimentally quite difﬁcult to distinguish the
contribution of each binding mode (diffuse, outer
sphere, and inner sphere) to the overall energetics of
RNA folding, despite fundamental differences in ion
hydration. This is a particular problem for the Mg ion,
which seems adept at all three binding modes. Most
solution methods used to study ion binding do not
distinguish the ion hydration state, forcing one to rely
on indirect arguments as to whether diffuse or site-
bound ions are being observed. Furthermore, since
diffuse ions do not follow a mass action law, a rigor-
ous thermodynamic interpretation of experimental
binding data is frequently not possible. Solution meth-
ods, including electron paramagnetic resonance, nmr,
and Raman spectroscopy, can be used to probe the
hydration state of the bound ions, but do not neces-
sarily give information about the stoichiometry of
binding.21 In contrast, x-ray crystallography can, at
high enough resolution, detect site-bound ions, and
their hydration state. However, crystallographic struc-
tures may not completely enumerate all of the poten-
tial binding sites in solution. Even for known crystal-
lographic ion binding sites, a satisfactory relationship
between these ions and solution binding studies has
not been made for any RNA. In addition, x-ray crys-
tallography does not give any information about dif-
fusely bound ions. These are formidable difﬁculties,
and in this review we will attempt to deﬁne these
problems more completely and suggest some ap-

It is important to realize that the desolvation of
Mg21 has a dominant effect on site binding (up to 460
kcal/mole for a completely desolvated ion34) that
must be compensated by other favorable interactions.
In fact, this premise forms the basis for the charac-
teristic selectivity of a site for particular ions, as
suggested by Eisenman more than 30 years ago.35,36
The Eisenman hypothesis is that the binding process
can be broken down into two steps: ﬁrst, stripping
water away from the ion and second, bringing the ion
into contact with the negative charges in the site. The
free energy of each of these processes can be approx-
imated with simple formulas. The cost of dehydrating
an ion (the so-called water to vacuum transfer free
energy) is given by the corrected Born equation,37
DGhyd 5 2 164(z2)/(r 1 0.7), where z is the charge
of the ion, r is the radius of the ion in Å, and the free
energy is given in units of kcal/mol at 25°C. Eisen-
man supposed that the dominant favorable force driv-
ing site binding arises from electrostatic interactions
of the ion with surrounding ligands in the binding
pocket. The free energy of these interactions is simply
given by Coulomb’s law, DGelec 5 332z(¥ qr)/(r
1 1.4), where Sqr is the sum of the charges on each
surrounding ligand, 1.4 is the radius of an oxygen
ligand, and the free energy is in units of kcal/mol at
25°C. It should be apparent that the relative binding
free energy for different ions at a particular site will
depend on two factors: ionic radius and the number of
phosphate ligands surrounding the ion at the site.
These terms are responsible for the ion size depen-
dence of site binding since smaller ions will interact
more strongly with the site but have a larger desolva-
tion penalty.35 The Eisenman hypothesis obviously
neglects a number of possible contributions to ion
binding such as desolvation of the binding pocket,
long-range Coulombic interactions, dielectric screen-
ing effects, atomic polarizability, dispersion forces,
charge-transfer effects, and a variety of other nonelec-
trostatic forces. However, the basic point of Eisen-
man’s proposal is that differences in hydration and
electrostatic free energies between different ions are
very large and are, thus, the primary determinants of
ion selectivity of site bound ions.

As mentioned above, diffuse ion binding repre-
sented by class II binding sites cannot be described
properly by simple reaction processes governed by the
laws of mass action.20 Therefore, analyses using bind-
ing polynomials provide no more than a purely phe-
nomenological view of the electrostatic interaction
between diffusely bound magnesium ions and RNA.
As a result, the properties of the counterion atmo-
sphere depend on the method used to characterize
diffuse binding. For example, the diffuse counterion
atmosphere is described as “bound” by equilibrium
dialysis experiments (due to the Donnan effect), but is
described as “free” in EPR measurements that directly
probe perturbations to ionic hydration.54 Despite this
limitation, binding polynomials do quantitatively de-
scribe the behavior of RNA in a variety of salt solu-
tions, although they cannot provide a basis for a more

The interaction of divalent ions with homopolymeric
RNAs [e.g., poly(A), poly(U), poly(A z U)] has been
extensively studied over the last 40 years. It was
apparent in very early studies that homopolymeric
RNAs are surrounded and stabilized by a delocalized
cloud of alkaline group I and II metal ions. Many
early studies also suggested that a class of these
counterions tightly associated with the helix such that
their apparent activity as ions was close to zero.56–58
In addition, it appeared that these counterions re-
mained associated with the nucleic acid even at very
low salt concentrations.58 Conductometric measure-
ments showed that Mg21, in particular, appeared to
bind stoichiometrically to poly(A) and poly(U).59 The
interaction of Mg21 with these RNAs was phenome-
nologically described by a single class of binding sites
The apparent binding constant Ka, was then estimated
by measuring the concentration of free Mg21 com-
pared to the total added Mg21 in the polyelectrolyte
solution. Table I contains the results of several studies
that quantify the binding of Mg21 to homopolymeric
RNAs using this approach. The value of Ka, for Mg21
binding to both single-stranded and double-stranded
RNA polymers was found to be about 104–102M21.60
As expected, Ka varied inversely with sodium con-
centration and extent of magnesium binding (i.e.,
binding is anticooperative), since each of these ions in
the vicinity of the RNA electrostatically repels addi-
tional Mg21. Therefore, at 0.01M sodium concentra-
tion Ka for poly(A z U) is 8.46 3 103M21, while at
0.1M sodium concentration Ka decreases to 1.98
3 102M21.60,62 These results agree with a variety of
data analyzed using the Scatchard method.61,63 Al-
though the anticooperativity results in curvature of the
Scatchard plot, in the limit of low Mg21 concentration
binding can be ﬁtted to a single class of binding sites
with an apparent binding constant of 1.4 3 102 to 6.8
3 103 at 0.01M sodium concentration and 25°C.61
The Scatchard plots further suggest that about one
Mg21 is bound to every two phosphates.61,63 Similar
results are found for long DNA polynucleotides.64
These and other data also show that multivalent cat-
ions can sometimes have a strong afﬁnity for partic-
ular base sequences on polynucleotides.65,66
The single class of ions described by these exper-
iments represent diffusely bound Mg21. A variety of
thermodynamic, kinetic, and spectroscopic experi-
ments show that Mg21 largely retains its hydration
shell and surrounds linear polynucleotides as a delo-
calized “ion cloud” without signiﬁcant site binding
(,10%).45,66–70 Although, 25Mg nmr data for DNA
suggests that the ions can be very tightly associated
with the helix at high ratios of Mg21 to DNA phos-
phate,71 the low value of the quadrupole coupling
constants suggests that the Mg ion remains hydrated.66
In any case, a general description of Mg21 interac-
tions with these RNAs must be based on a theory that
treats the long-range electrostatic effects involved in
diffuse binding rather than binding polynomials that
assume only discrete binding sites.
Electrostatic models based on counterion conden-
Monte Carlo methods, and the hypernetted chain
equation have been used to successfully predict the
thermodynamic behavior of polynucleotides in diva-
lent salt solutions.19,32,47,72–75 Each of these models
describes salt effects using the “standard model” in
which the nucleic acid is described as an inﬁnitely
long, uniformly charged cylinder immersed in a sol-
vent described as a structureless continuum.73 Al-
though they differ in their description of the radial
distribution of small ions around the nucleic acid,
each of these models clearly shows how Mg21 effects
on polyelectrolytes are governed by a population of
diffusely bound ions. However, the “standard model”
does not explicitly account for the detailed molecular
structures and charge distributions of the polyelectro-
lyte. As a result, these simple cylindrical models are
of limited use in more complex RNA systems de-
The interaction of divalent metal ions with large het-
eropolymeric RNAs is more complex. These RNAs
can adopt compact, globular structures in which the
negatively charged phosphates are very closely
packed. The electrostatic repulsions among the phos-
phates are still compensated by a diffusely bound
counterion atmosphere. However, when the RNA
folds into its native conformation, polar groups on the
molecule, including the phosphate oxygens, 29-hy-
droxyls, base carbonyls, and transition state oxyan-
ions, can be speciﬁcally arranged to act as coordinat-
ing ligands for a particular metal ion.12 These nega-
tively charged pockets are capable of displacing some
or all of the shell of tightly bound waters surrounding
an ion, resulting in a speciﬁc site binding.
Our current understanding of ionic effects in large
RNA systems, in both thermodynamic and structural
terms, is largely based on classical studies of tRNA.76
The importance of cations in the folding of yeast
tRNAs was evident nearly 30 years ago when it was
shown that Mg21 could restore the amino acid accep-
tor activity of denatured tRNA.8 Soon afterwards it
was shown that a number of univalent and divalent
cations could effectively renature tRNA.7 Systematic
thermal melting proﬁles under a variety of ionic con-
ditions characterized the inﬂuence of both univalent
and divalent salts on tRNA conformational equilib-

However, many studies of Mg21 binding to tRNA
did not uncouple ion binding from RNA folding as
required for analysis of the binding polynomial using
Eq. (11). At low monovalent ion concentrations,
Mg21 binding to tRNA is cooperative.78–82,84 That is
to say, the binding of the ﬁrst divalent ion facilitates
binding of subsequent ions. It has been shown at low
monovalent ion concentrations that divalent ion bind-
ing is coupled to a cation induced conformational
change from a nonnative “extended” form to the na-
tive form.9,52 Formation of the more compact native
structure not only electrostatically attracts the diffuse
ion atmosphere, but can also create site binding pock-
ets for Mg21. Under these conditions, the observed
cooperativity of divalent ion binding reﬂects differ-
ences in ion afﬁnity between the extended and native
forms rather than the inherent divalent ion binding
properties of native tRNA.53 In contrast, Mg21 bind-
ing to native tRNA at high monovalent salt concen-
tive.52,53,80,83,85 Under these conditions, the high Na1
concentration stabilizes the native tRNA fold and
Mg21 simply stabilizes this structure.9,86,87 Thus, for
an experiment to measure Mg21 binding afﬁnity in-
dependent of any structural changes in the nucleic
acid, it is necessary to perform the experiment under
conditions in which the RNA is folded in the absence
of Mg21. This could involve adding another counte-
rion, such as Na1 or a polyamine,52,53 or even reduc-
ing the temperature80,83 to promote RNA folding.

The location of the site-bound magnesium ions has
been inferred from crystallographic structures of yeast
tRNAPhe.88–91 The precise crystallographic position
and binding mode of divalent ions depends on the
crystal form, monoclinic91 or orthorhombic.88 The
reﬁned structure of the monoclinic (2.5 Å resolution)
has ﬁve localized magnesium ions, while the orthor-
hombic form (2.7 Å resolution) has four localized
magnesiums. The positions of the magnesium ions in
the reﬁned monoclinic form of the crystal shown in
Figure 4 are representative of both crystal forms91:
three magnesium binding sites are found in the central
core region around the bend of the L-shaped molecule
(near the TCC loop, dihydrouridine loop, and dihy-
drouridine stem); two other magnesium sites are bur-
ied in the major groove of the anticodon stem.
The crystallographic magnesium ions are de-
scribed as being “coordinated,” either directly or via a
bridging water, to nearby anionic phosphate oxygens.
Generally, one or two phosphate oxygens displace
water and are directly ligated to the ion forming an
“inner sphere” complex with bound waters remaining
as the other ligands. A typical “inner sphere” complex
is the Mg21 bound to the anionic oxygens of G20 and
A21 shown in Figure 5. In other cases, the Mg21 is
entirely coordinated by water molecules forming an
“outer sphere” complex like those found in the anti-

identify the Mg ions localized in the crystal structures
with the class I sites found in solution, there is no
direct evidence to support this assumption.

ing.93,94 This site may be related to a Mg21 ion

If Mg21 binding data can be largely explained by
a single class of diffusely bound ions, how can the site
speciﬁc ions seen in crystals be explained? The local-
ization of a cation to a speciﬁc region may result from
a large local electrostatic potential arising from the
many charged phosphate groups throughout the mol-
ecule. Because tRNA has an irregular shape and
charge distribution, the electrostatic potential will
vary from site to site around the molecule.26 Regions
with larger negative electrostatic potentials will have
larger accumulation of cations. It has therefore been
suggested that electrostatic forces can enhance the
afﬁnity of divalent ions for particular regions of the
RNA even without any of the local interactions in-
volved in site binding.54,98,99 As such, we expect to
ﬁnd divalent ions around the closely packed phos-
phates in the central core of the tRNA. This notion is
supported by the observed positions of magnesium in
the crystal structure of yeast tRNAPhe, as well as
solution studies in which the manganese-dependent
ﬂuorescence properties of 4-thiouracil (near the core)
compared to the Y-base (in the anticodon loop) sug-
gest that divalent ions preferentially accumulate
A purely electrostatic interpretation of ion binding
also suggests that monovalent cations may substitute for
divalent cations in their structural role. Indeed, the af-
ﬁnity of divalent ions for tRNA has been shown to
decrease as sodium concentration is increased.54 In ad-
dition, while magnesium does markedly stabilize the
tertiary structure of tRNA, it is not absolutely essential
for folding. Numerous studies, including thermal dena-
turation,9 calorimetry,100 and nmr,86,87 have shown that
tRNA adopts its native fold at high univalent salt con-
centrations and that the addition of magnesium does not
induce any new tertiary structures.

Taken together, these types of data have been used
to argue that site bound Mg21 ions do not play a
signiﬁcant role in the structure of most tRNAs in
solution.54,98,99 In this view, the dominant interaction
is the nonspeciﬁc binding of diffuse Mg ions with the
Mg, much like interactions with poly(A z U)
or DNA. That said, under certain conditions some
tRNAs do seem to have one or two high afﬁnity Mg21
binding sites at high monovalent salt concentra-
tions.52,53 Although they may not be required to form
the native tertiary structure, these tightly bound ions
do play an important role in stabilizing the structure.

are the simplest of these small RNAs. Magnesium
binding to a number of hairpins has been probed using

In contrast to the hairpin and pseudoknot struc-
tures, the more complex folded tertiary structure of
GACG RNA, a 58-nucleotide fragment of the large
subunit of rRNA, is preferentially stabilized by spe-
ciﬁcally site bound Mg ions.16,106 Melting experi-
ments on this RNA show that it is able to form its
native tertiary fold at high monovalent salt concentra-
tion.107 Analysis of the binding polynomials for Mg21
at 1.6M NH4Cl concentration show that this tertiary
structure is strongly stabilized by two site bound Mg
ions with afﬁnities of about 1 3 1023M21.106 As
expected for site bound ions, the afﬁnity of divalent
ions for both of these sites depends strongly on the
ionic radius. At 1.6M NH4Cl, diffusely bound Mg
ions do not have a signiﬁcant part in preferentially
stabilizing the folded form.16
The binding of Mg21 to the 120-nucleotide Esch-
erichia coli 5S RNA has been explored using 25Mg
nmr.108,109 Using this technique, a large number of
Mg ions (about one ion per phosphate) are found to be
associated with 5S RNA. The binding sites fall into
two weakly bound classes: one that is readily dis-
placed by monovalent ions and another that is not as
effectively displaced by Na1 or K1.108 Measurement
of the nuclear quadrupole coupling constant in 0.2M
NaCl shows that up to 80 of these fully hydrated Mg
ions are loosely associated with the RNA (Ka 5 2.36
3 102M21), while there is no evidence of any strongly
bound inner sphere complexes.109
It is interesting to compare these data to the crys-
tallographic structure of a 62-nucleotide domain of
the E. coli 5S RNA and a duplex dodecamer encom-
passing an important internal loop structure of this
RNA.110 Both of these crystal structures show metal
ions bound in a narrowed major groove. The 1.5 Å
resolution map of the dodecamer reveals ﬁve Mg ions
that share hydration shells and make direct inner
sphere contacts to anionic phosphate oxygens lining
the groove (Figure 7). Thus, it is again evident that the
correlation between solution studies and the crystal-
lographic data on metal binding is poor.
Recently, ribozymes derived from the self-splicing
Tetrahymena thermophila group I intron have become
a paradigm for understanding the effects of Mg21 on
the stability of large RNAs. Shortened forms of the
413 nucleotide intron, like the 388 nucleotide L-21
Sca I fragment, have sequence speciﬁc endoribo-
nuclease activity in the presence of Mg21 and a bound
thought to play two distinct roles in this ribozyme:
structural stabilization and enzymatic chemistry.111
There are very few thermodynamic data on Mg21
binding to the Tetrahymena ribozyme so little is
known about the relative importance of diffuse and
site bound ions in its stability. Nevertheless, many
insights into the role of Mg21 in RNA folding have
been gained from other analyses of this system.
Folding of the Tetrahymena ribozyme depends
strongly on Mg21 concentration.112,113 Like tRNA,
the tertiary folding of the L-21 Sca I fragment is
accompanied by a large uptake of Mg21 ions. Hill
analyses of Eq. (21) for L-21 Sca I ribozyme folding
suggested that up to eight Mg21 ions (Ka ; 1.5–2.0
3 103M21) are involved in the highly cooperative
folding process.112,113 Also as seen with tRNA, low
concentrations of sodium inhibit Mg21 induced fold-
ing of the fragment. In contrast to tRNA, however, a
high concentration of sodium (from 0.05 to 1.6M)
alone is not sufﬁcient to stabilize the active tertiary
structure,112 although, high concentrations of sodium
can stabilize speciﬁc subdomains within the overall
structure.114 These data suggest that Mg21 may sta-
bilize the active tertiary structure of the ribozyme by
binding to speciﬁc sites, although diffusely bound
ions still play a crucial role.
Equilibrium folding of the L-21 Sca I fragment has
been studied using free radical cleavage of ribose to
map solvent accessibility of the entire RNA backbone
as a function of Mg21 concentration.112,115 These data
show that ribozyme folding proceeds as two separate
but coupled folding transitions with different Mg21
requirements.115 The ﬁrst transition involves the in-
dependent folding of the 160 nucleotide “P4–P6”
domain coupled to strong Mg21 binding ([Mg]1/2
5 0.7 mM). The remainder of the catalytic core (the
P1–P2.1/P3–P9 domain) folds in the second transition
with slightly weaker Mg21 binding ([Mg]1/2 5 0.85
mM). The second transition depends strongly on the
presence of the intact, folded “P4–P6” domain. These
data are supported by more rapid kinetic measure-
ments114,116,117 that estimate the coupled magnesium
association constant Ka, for the ﬁrst step to be about 4
3 103M21 and Ka for the second step to be about 2
3 103M21.114,117 Thus, Mg21 is important not only in
the overall thermodynamic stabilization of the RNA,
but can also inﬂuence the kinetic pathways involved
in the folding process by stabilizing RNA domains
The 56-nucleotide P5abc subdomain is a particu-
larly interesting region of the P4–P6 domain. In both
the intact intron and the isolated P4–P6 domain, spe-
ciﬁc positions in the P5abc subdomain are protected
from free radical cleavage at very low Mg21 concen-
tration.112,118 Magnesium binds to the subdomain co-
operatively with a Hill coefﬁcient of about 3. In
addition, proton nmr data show that the native sec-
ondary structure of this subdomain will form only in
the presence of Mg21.119 It has been suggested that
this region forms a scaffolding for Mg21 binding that
stabilizes the entire domain.120 However, the energet-
ics of Mg21 binding must still be resolved.

RNA has long been known to depend on the type and

concentration of counterions in solution. The strong
electrostatic repulsion between closely packed phos-
phate anions on the backbone tends to unwind the
RNA. Divalent cations, like magnesium, are crucial in
reducing these repulsions and thus stabilizing the
folded conformation of the RNA. We have developed
here a picture of how these cations can stabilize a
particular folded form of RNA.
Conceptually, magnesium can interact with RNA
in several distinct ways, including diffuse binding,
outer sphere and inner sphere complexes, largely dis-
tinguished by their hydration properties. In diffuse
binding, fully hydrated divalent ions interact with
nucleic acids via nonspeciﬁc long-range electrostatic
interactions. These types of interactions are responsi-
ble for the “delocalized” counterion atmosphere sur-
rounding all nucleic acids. The strong electrostatic
ﬁeld surrounding RNA can result in the accumulation
of diffusely bound ions in pockets of negative elec-
trostatic potential created by the irregular shape of the
molecular surface. However, in some cases, ions be-
come trapped in these electrostatic potential wells
such that their thermal translational energy is not
sufﬁcient for them to continue their random motion.
These electrostatically site bound ions form an “outer
sphere” complex and are thought to retain all but their
innermost hydration layer. In strong site binding, di-
valent counterions interact with anionic ligands ar-
ranged in the RNA fold to act as speciﬁc coordinating
ligands for the metal ion. These ligands are able to
dehydrate the ion. Although this picture is based on a
variety of experimental data, the distinction between
diffuse and site binding in solution must still be made
largely on energetic grounds. Unfortunately, the cor-
relation between the conceptual view of ion binding
and the energetics of Mg21 binding in solution is
poorly understood. For example, it is particularly dif-
ﬁcult to distinguish the energetics of diffuse binding
from outer sphere complex formation. In addition
there is no satisfactory relationship between crystallo-
graphically observed ions and solution binding studies.
We have presented here a physical framework that
can be used to understand Mg21–RNA interactions.
The overall stabilization of RNA by Mg21 is given by
the term DGMg, which represents the free energy of
transferring RNA from a reference univalent salt so-
lution to a mixed salt solution. This term has favor-
able energetic contributions from diffuse binding
partitioned. Each of these terms has a strong coulom-
bic contribution to binding; however, the term DGb
is also characterized by substantial changes in ion
solvation and other nonelectrostatic contributions. We

ploited to experimentally distinguish between these
As classically shown for tRNA, the energetic con-
tribution of Mg21 to RNA stability can be estimated
using analyses of binding polynomials. However, in
order to distinguish different classes of bound ions
two criteria must be met: ﬁrst, ion binding must be
uncoupled from structural transitions in the RNA, and
second, there must be a signiﬁcant difference in the
binding constant between the classes of sites. These
requirements limit our ability to interpret ion binding
data in a number of systems. Nevertheless, in certain
tRNAGlu,53 and the GACG rRNA fragment,106 these
types of studies show that site bound ions can play an
important role in RNA stability. However, these stud-
ies also emphasize the crucial role of diffusely bound
ions. Since these diffusely bound ions do not bind to
speciﬁc sites per se, binding polynomials provide only
a phenomenological description of these interactions
with RNA. As such, alternative methods to character-
ize diffuse ion binding must be developed. Diffuse
binding can only be described rigorously by a model
which accounts for long range electrostatic interac-
tions, like Poisson–Boltzmann, counterion condensa-
tion, or Monte Carlo based theory.
A general model for describing the properties of
RNA in solution will also require a rigorous treatment
of site binding that includes both the electrostatic and

To fully understand the role of divalent ions in RNA
stability, theoretical models describing electrostatic
forces in complex nucleic acid systems must be devel-
oped. Many theoretical models describing salt effects in
nucleic acid systems are based on elegant analytical
solutions to the classical electrostatic equations, but
make severe assumptions about the shape, charge distri-
bution, and dielectric properties of the nucleic acid as
well as the dielectric behavior, ion structure, and ion
distribution of the surrounding ion atmosphere. As dis-
cussed above, the “standard model,” in which the nu-
cleic acid is described as an inﬁnitely long, uniformly
charged cylinder, has been developed using counterion
condensation theory, Poisson–Boltzmann theory, Monte
Carlo methods, and the hypernetted chain equation.
These models have been remarkably successful in de-
scribing the interactions of divalent ions with rod-like
homopolymeric RNAs. However, since these simple
models do not explicitly account for the detailed molec-
ular structure and charge distribution of the RNA, they
cannot make quantitative thermodynamic predictions for
more complex RNA structures. Clearly, a model that
speciﬁcally relates the molecule’s three-dimensional
shape and charge distribution to its free energy is needed
to describe magnesium ion effects in RNA systems.
